__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_0__: SaaS Orodha ya Due Diligence: Buyer-Side Guide (2026) | Churn Lens
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_16__: 19 critical checks before you buy a SaaS business. Organized by risk domain — churn, concentration, revenue quality, unit economics, technical debt, and legal.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_17__: Every SaaS acquisition looks good in the CIM. The deal memo hides the landmines. This checklist forces you to look at each one — before you sign.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_1__: Complete buyer-side SaaS due diligence checklist. Cover churn, concentration risk, revenue quality, unit economics, and technical debt before you buy.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_21__: What percentage of customers cancel each month? Benchmark against sector medians. Anything above 5% monthly churn for B2B SaaS requires a structural reason (clear intent, seasonal business). Ask for the raw data, not a calculated metric.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_24__: NRR = (Starting MRR + Expansion – Churn) / Starting MRR. An NRR >100% means expansion offsets churn — the asset grows itself. Below 85% means the company is slowly dying. Demand the calculation method.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_27__: A single churn number hides cohort decay. Newer cohorts that churn faster than old ones indicate product-market fit erosion, competitive pressure, or a degrading onboarding experience. Request cohort tables by quarter of acquisition.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_2__: SaaS Orodha ya Due Diligence: Buyer-Side Guide (2026)
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_30__: Involuntary churn (failed payments, expired cards) is fixable. Voluntary churn (cancelled because the product wasn't valuable enough) is structural. If voluntary churn exceeds 60% of total, the value prop needs work — and you'll inherit that work.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_34__: If the top 5 customers represent more than 30% of MRR, you're buying a services business with a SaaS wrapper — not a product company. Losing one customer permanently changes the valuation basis. Calculate it yourself from raw CSV data.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_37__: If 70% of MRR comes from "Enterprise" tier customers, the revenue base is fragile during an acquisition transition. Enterprise customers churn less but churn harder when they do (more revenue per lost logo).
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_3__: 19-point buyer-side SaaS due diligence checklist covering churn, concentration, revenue quality, unit economics, and technical debt.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_40__: Is revenue concentrated in one vertical or geography? A single GDPR regulatory shift, currency fluctuation, or sector downturn can wipe 40%+ of MRR overnight.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_44__: Flat or declining MRR over 12 months is the #1 deal-killer signal we see in Churn Lens analyses. Ask for month-by-month MRR for the last 24 months and look for inflection points (price changes, product launches, churn waves).
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_47__: Expansion revenue sounds good until you realize it's masking customers downgrading plans. Contraction (downgrades + plan reductions) tells you if the core value prop is eroding. Request downgrade events separately from churn.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_4__: SaaS Orodha ya Due Diligence: Buyer-Side Guide (2026)
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_50__: The average time between signup and first payment tells you whether the product delivers quick value. Long payment velocity combined with high churn is a double signal: the product doesn't hook users fast enough.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_54__: A 3:1 LTV/CAC is the SaaS baseline. Below 3:1 means the company loses money on each customer over its lifetime. Above 5:1 means they may be under-investing in growth. Calculate from raw data — don't accept the seller's blended number.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_57__: How many months of gross margin does it take to recover customer acquisition costs? Over 18 months is risky for a bootstrapped acquisition. Under 6 months and the pricing may be too low.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_60__: A declining gross margin trend signals infrastructure bloat, rising cloud costs, or increasing support load per customer. Stable or improving margins are a green flag.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_64__: Check the dependency tree age, upgrade cadence, and whether there's automated testing. A stovepiped codebase with no CI/CD and a single developer key-man risk can add 6–12 months to your post-acquisition integration plan.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_67__: Is the product running because one person knows the infrastructure? A founder-run micro-SaaS that's never been documented will crater within 90 days of the founder leaving. Look at commit authorship distribution.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_70__: A serverless app tightly coupled to a single cloud provider's services is hard to migrate. Factor migration costs into your valuation model.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_74__: GDPR, CCPA, and sector-specific regulations change ownership obligations if you acquire. Check whether the current ToS allows assignment, and whether data processing agreements are in place with every customer.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_77__: Contractors without signed IP assignments, open-source license violations in the codebase, or trademark gaps in key markets can all surface post-close. Verify all contributor agreements.
__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_80__: Every SaaS the business relies on (Stripe, AWS, SendGrid, OpenAI) is a risk if the plan is unsignable. Check change-of-control clauses in vendor contracts.
